coredata.dc:title: Chiral carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for rapid chiral recognition of isoleucine enantiomers
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Xiudan
Song, Jiying
Wu, Qi
Lv, Haitao
coredata.dc:description: Chiral recognition is always a significant and challenging work in analytical chemistry. A fluorescent chiral recognition method based on chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) towards isoleucine (Ile) enantiomers was developed in this work. CCQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using l-cysteine as chiral source. The fluorescence intensity of CCQDs enhanced obviously in the presence of L-Ile, but had no observable change in the presence of D-Ile. The response speed of this chiral sensing system is fast, Ile enantiomers can be discriminated by CCQDs within 5 min, the enantioselectivity (IL/ID) can reach up to 2.2. Good linearity for detecting L-Ile was obtained over the concentration range from 0 to 30 mM with a LOD of 0.29 mM. The fluorescence intensity also increased linearly with the enantiomeric percentages of L-Ile in the mixture of Ile enantiomers. Thus, the developed method not only can achieve quantitative detection of L-Ile but also can determine the enantiomeric percentage in racemates. The chiral recognition mechanism can be explained by the difference in binding energy and interaction types between D-Ile and L-Ile with CCQDs by molecular modeling. The current method was applied in detecting L-Ile in real samples of functional drinks, the detection results were in consistent with the results obtained from high performance liquid chromatography, and the recoveries of standard addition were also satisfactory, which verified the reliability of the developed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral recognition
Fluorescence
Chiral carbon quantum dots
l-isoleucine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enantioselective fluorescent sensing performance of CCQDs
Determination of L-Ile in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901766), the Talents of High Level Scientific Research Foundation, Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016), and the Support Plan on Science and Technology for Youth Innovation of Universities in Shandong Province (No. 2019KJM002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and DLS data (b) of the synthesized CCQDs.

Characterization of CCQDs. XRD pattern (a); FT-IR spectrogram (b); XPS survey spectrum (c); High resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (d), O 1s (e), N 1s (f) and S 2p (g); Absorption spectra of CCQDs and L-Cys (g); Emission spectra of CCQDs at different excitation wavelengths (i).
Excitation and emission spectra of CCQDs (a); PL spectra change of CCQDs with Ile enantiomers (b).

The fluorescence intensity ratio (IL/ID) of CCQDs induced by different kinds of chiral amino acids (a); The enantioselectivity change with the prolong of time (b); The influence of pH on the PL intensity of CCQDs (c); The influence of pH on the enantioselectivity of CCQDs (d).

Fluorescence spectra of CCQDs with the addition of various concentrations of L-Ile (a); The linear relationship between fluorescence enhancement (I–I0) and L-/D-Ile concentrations in the range from 0 to 30 mM, I0 and I are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of L-/D-Ile, respectively (b); Fluorescence spectra of CCQDs in the presence of racemic solution with different enantiomeric percentage of L-Ile (c); The linear curve between I–I0 and the enantiomeric percentage of L-Ile in the range of 10–100% (d).
The molecular modeling for the interaction between D-Ile and CCQDs (Left), and L-Ile and CCQDs (Right) from three different chiral recognition regions (A, B and C).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CCQDs with the addition of various concentrations of L-Ile in energy drinks; (b) Chromatograms of L-Ile standard at concentration of 5 mM with injected volume of 20 μL (a) and real sample of energy drinks injected with 20 μL without any pretreatment (b).
Binding energies of D-/L-Ile and CCQDs in three chiral recognition regions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: E: Binding energy; ΔE: Binding energy difference between D-Ile and L-Ile enantiomers.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of L-Ile in supplemented energy drinks detected by current method.

head.ce:title: Chiral carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for rapid chiral recognition of isoleucine enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. No. 700, Changcheng Road, Chengyang District, Qingdao, Shandong Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral recognition is always a significant and challenging work in analytical chemistry. A fluorescent chiral recognition method based on chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) towards isoleucine (Ile) enantiomers was developed in this work. CCQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using l-cysteine as chiral source. The fluorescence intensity of CCQDs enhanced obviously in the presence of L-Ile, but had no observable change in the presence of D-Ile. The response speed of this chiral sensing system is fast, Ile enantiomers can be discriminated by CCQDs within 5 min, the enantioselectivity (IL/ID) can reach up to 2.2. Good linearity for detecting L-Ile was obtained over the concentration range from 0 to 30 mM with a LOD of 0.29 mM. The fluorescence intensity also increased linearly with the enantiomeric percentages of L-Ile in the mixture of Ile enantiomers. Thus, the developed method not only can achieve quantitative detection of L-Ile but also can determine the enantiomeric percentage in racemates. The chiral recognition mechanism can be explained by the difference in binding energy and interaction types between D-Ile and L-Ile with CCQDs by molecular modeling. The current method was applied in detecting L-Ile in real samples of functional drinks, the detection results were in consistent with the results obtained from high performance liquid chromatography, and the recoveries of standard addition were also satisfactory, which verified the reliability of the developed method.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is the nature of life. Biological molecules such as amino acids, proteins, and nucleic acids all have chiral characteristics, thus biological and chemical reactions in life are closely related to chiral recognition. Enantiomers of drugs often have different biological activities, pharmacology and toxicology in the chiral environment of organism [1]. Chiral recognition plays an important role in understanding the biological and pharmacological mechanisms in life, and is always the research topic in analytical chemistry.

Amino acids are the basic building components of proteins showing important biological functions. l-amino acids can be absorbed and transformed into proteins by human body, while d-amino acids cannot [2]. It has been reported that abnormal levels of amino acids in human body are associated with some diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and schizophrenia [3–5]. Essential amino acids must be intake directly from foods, thus l-amino acids are widely used as food additives [6]. But l-amino acids are easily racemized during the food processing, which reduces the effective content of nutrients in food [7]. Therefore, chiral recognition of amino acids is of great significance for medical research, disease diagnosis and food analysis [8].

The physical and chemical properties of enantiomers are almost identical. Therefore, chiral recognition is always a challenging work. At present, the mature chiral recognition methods mainly depend on chromatographic separation [9–11]. Chromatography has high accuracy, but the detection process is time-consuming and organic solvents or gas are always required, which increases the detection cost. Enantioselective fluorescent recognition with high sensitivity and simple operation process, has been increasingly applied in chiral recognition [12,13]. In the past decades, a series of chiral fluorescent probes including organic molecules, supramolecules, nanomaterials and porous materials have been synthesized and applied in chiral recognition and detection [13–17].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with excellent solubility, photoluminescence properties, photobleaching resistance, low toxicity and biocompatibility, which have been widely used in biological imaging and fluorescence sensing [18]. Chiral CQDs (CCQDs) were synthesized in the year of 2016 by Suzuki and coworkers [19]. After that, CCQDs have become a hot topic in various research fields such as antimicrobial activity [20], growth-promoting effects [21], long-term imaging [22], chiral induction function [23–25], asymmetric catalysis [26] and electrochemical chiral recognition performance [27–29]. However, unlike achiral CQDs which have been widely used in fluorescence sensing, there are a few reports on the applications of CCQDs in enantioselective fluorescent sensing. In 2019, CCQDs were used as fluorescent probes for chiral recognition of lysine enantiomers [30]. The CCQDs were synthesized by post-modification of l-cysteine (L-Cys) on CQDs, and exhibited an enantioselective response towards l-lysine with a LOD value of 0.30 mM. Moreover, the CCQDs were fabricated into nanopaper-based sensor for visual inspection of l-lysine. In 2020, Gao and coworkers synthesized CCQDs using citric acid and l-aspartic acid as carbon sources. The CCQDs were applied in chiral recognition of lysine enantiomers through Sn2+ as medium [31]. The CCQD–Sn system behaved as an off-on fluorescent probe for enantioselectively sensing l-lysine with LOD of 3.34 μM. Recently, Kong's group reported the application of ionized CCQDs in chiral recognition of nonaromatic amino alcohols including 2-aminobutan-1-ol and 2-aminopropan-1-ol enantiomers using Cu2+ as medium [32], which further enriched the types of CCQDs and expanded their application scope. At present, only lysine enantiomers achieved enantioselective fluorescent recognition by CCQDs among all the chiral amino acids. There are still extensive room for developing CCQDs-based chiral recognition systems towards amino acids.

Isoleucine (Ile) is one of the essential amino acids in human and animals, has been widely used as food additives. Thus, chiral recognition and determination of L-Ile is of significance in food industries. Here, a fluorescent chiral recognition method towards Ile enantiomers is established. CCQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using L-Cys and citric acid as precursors. L-Ile can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of CCQDs, but D-Ile has no obvious influence on the fluorescence of CCQDs, thus a fluorescent chiral recognition system can be established. Under the optimized conditions, the enantioselectivity (IL/ID) can reach up to 2.2. It is worth noting that differently from the previously reported methods [31–33] where fluorescence signal difference comes from the different degrees of fluorescence intensity recovery followed after quenching by metal ions, the chiral sensing system developed in this work is simpler by directly investigating the change in fluorescence intensity without the need of any other indicator due to the good chiral recognition performance of the synthesized CCQDs. Liner relationships between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of L-Ile as well as the enantiomeric percentage of L-Ile are established. The chiral recognition mechanism is investigated by molecular modeling. The practical applicability of the developed method is tested by determining L-Ile in real sample of energy drinks.

Amino acids isomers including D-/L-Cys, D-/L-Ala, D-/L-Glu, D-/L-Thr, D-/L-Met, D-/L-Leu, D-/L-Ile, D-/L-Val, D-/L-Pro, D-/L-Arg, D-/L-His, D-/L-Phe, D-/L-Ser, D-/L-Gln, D-/L-Asn, D-/L-Asp, D-/L-Lys, D-/L-Trp were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, phosphoric acid, boric acid, acetic acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100 F field emission TEM (JEOL, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra was acquired on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray crystal diffraction (XRD) was performed on D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on a VG ESCALAB 210 instrument (Thermo, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential analysis was performed on Zetasizer Nano ZSE nanoparticle size analyzer (Malvern, Britain). The photoluminescent spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA).

CCQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and L-Cys as precursors. The detailed synthesis process was as follows: 2.0 g citric acid and 0.5 g L-Cys were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a nylon membrane (220 nm) and the light-yellow supernatant was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 3 days. Finally, CCQDs were obtained by freeze drying.

1.0 mg mL−1 of CCQDs and 0.1 mol L−1 L-/D-Ile are prepared as the stock solution. For the detection of L-/D-Ile, 50 μL CCQDs and different volumes of L-/D-Ile stock solution were mixed and diluted by Britton–Robinson buffer (pH = 7.4) to the final volume of 4.0 mL. After 5 min, the mixture was subjected to fluorescence spectrometer and the fluorescence spectra (λex = 360 nm) were measured.

The morphology of the synthesized CCQDs was characterized by TEM. From TEM image (Fig. 1 a) we can see that CCQDs are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles. The size distribution is figured out by measuring the size of one hundred of particles in the TEM images. Particle size statistics show the diameters of CCQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 2–6 nm with an average size of 3.9 nm. The polydispersity of the prepared CCQDs was also investigated via DLS. The hydrodynamic size of CCQDs was observed to be an average size of 7 nm (Fig. 1b). The increased particle size obtained via DLS is normally expected, which is explained on the basis of formation of electrical double layer on the particle, as well as the path hindrance created with overlapped nanoparticles while conducting the scattering experiment [34]. The CCQDs had a negative zeta potential (−22.8 mV) as a result of the carboxylic surface functional groups. The large values of the zeta potential indicate a stable colloid system. The HRTEM image (bottom inset of Fig. 1a) clearly displays the lattice fringes of CCQDs, which indicates that the CCQDs are highly crystalline in nature. To better understand the crystallinity of CCQDs, XRD characterization was carried out. There is a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.5° (Fig. 2 a), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon with lattice spacing of 0.41 nm [35].

The chemical compositions of CCQDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS. Fig. 2b presents the FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized CCQDs, the characteristic peaks at 3136 and 3415 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. The small peak at 2560 cm−1 is the stretching vibration band of S–H. The characteristic peaks at 1714, 1395 and 1216 cm−1 are attributed to CO, C–N and C–O, respectively. The XPS survey spectrum of CCQDs in Fig. 2c reveals the presence of C, N, O and S signals without any other impurities. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum in Fig. 2d can be deconvoluted into six peaks corresponding to CC (283.9 eV), C–C (284.4 eV), C–N (284.9 eV), C–O (286.1 eV), CO (288.0 eV), and O–CO (288.7 eV). The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2e) could be divided into two components, corresponding to C–O (531.0 eV) and CO (532.2 eV). The last weak hump at 535.5 eV can be assigned to a π to π∗ shake up peak, which is caused by the collisions of the shared electrons with the ejected core line electrons in the π orbital [36,37]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum in Fig. 2f contains two dominant components including pyrazine-like C–N (400.1 eV) and amino N (401.4 eV). The XPS spectrum of S 2p displayed two major deconvoluted peaks at 163.4 and 164.5 eV that correspond to the thiol group (S–H) of L-Cys molecule and C–S covalent bond, respectively.

Optical properties of CCQDs were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. The UV–vis spectrum of CCQDs shows three absorption peaks at 350, 247 and 210 nm, respectively (Fig. 2h). The absorption band at 247 nm is attributed to π−π∗ transition of conjugated system in CCQDs. The peak at 350 nm originates from the n-π∗ transition of the carboxyl, amino or other moieties. The strong absorption peak at 210 nm is due to the preservation of L-Cys precursor compared to the UV–vis absorption spectrum of pure L-Cys. The fluorescence emission spectra of CCQDs under different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2i. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity and peak positions depend on the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelengths varied from 300 to 400 nm, the PL intensity first increased and then decreased, the wavelength of emission peaks relevantly red shifted from 448 to 467 nm, which indicates that the as-synthesized CCQDs have excitation-dependent PL performance. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of CCQDs is at 350 and 453 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enantioselective fluorescent sensing performance of CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The enantioselectivity denoted as the PL intensity ratio of l-isomer and d-isomer (IL/ID) towards various chiral amino acids was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, there is no significant difference between L and D isomers of another 17 chiral amino acids except Ile whose IL/ID reaches 2.2 when the concentration of the detected amino acids is 10 mmol L−1. As shown in Fig. 3b, the addition of L-Ile leads to a significant enhancement of the PL intensity of CCQDs whereas almost no observable change is obtained from adding of D-Ile. The PL signal difference between L-Ile and D-Ile roots in their different stereoscopic interactions with CCQDs. The emission enhancement effect of L-Ile may be due to the chiral matching interaction between L-Ile and CCQDs which restricts intra-molecular vibration and rotation of functionalized units in CCQDs and reduces the non-radiative relaxation of excited states [30].

The chiral recognition ability of CCQDs towards Ile enantiomers under different incubation time was tested. As shown in Fig. 4b, chiral recognition can be achieved fast without needing long incubation time compared with other methods [38]. This may be due to the good water solubility of CCQDs, and thus the rapid mass transfer and equilibrium of Ile enantiomers from solution to the surface of CCQDs. With the extension of time, the enantioselectivity has no obvious change. To improve detection efficiency, 5 min is chosen as the incubation time of CCQDs and D-/L-Ile before PL measurement in all quantitative analysis experiments.

The chiral recognition performance of CCQDs under different pH conditions was explored. Fig. 4c shows the PL intensity of CCQDs dispersed in different pH buffer solutions. We can see that pH has certain influence on the PL performance of CCQDs, with the increase of basicity, the PL intensity of CCQDs enhances. However, pH has slight impact on the chiral recognition ability of CCQDs, the enantioselectivity (IL/ID) has no obvious change with the variation of pH as shown in Fig. 4d. Thus, physiological pH value with satisfactory enantioselectivity is chosen as the testing condition.

The relationship between PL enhancement and the concentrations of L-Ile was investigated. As presented in Fig. 5 a, the PL intensity at 456 nm (exc. 360 nm) gradually enhances with the increase of the concentration of L-Ile. PL intensity enhancement (I–I0) has a good linear relationship with the concentration of L-Ile in the range of 0–30 mM (Fig. 5b), the corresponding linear regression equation is I–I0 = 12.375 c (L-Ile) + 0.525, R2 = 0.9963. The LOD for L-Ile is calculated to be 0.29 mM. Whereas the addition of D-Ile with corresponding concentration to CCQDs induces no significant fluorescence change.

To further explore the enantioselective sensing capability of CCQDs, a series of PL measurements were carried out by adjusting the enantiomeric percentage of L-Ile. As shown in Fig. 5c, the PL intensity enhances with the increased proportion of L-Ile in racemized mixture. Moreover, a good linear relationship between I–I0 and the percentage of L-Ile (0–100%) can be obtained as shown in Fig. 5d, the corresponding linear regression equation is I–I0 = 1.373 L-Ile% + 3.163, R2 = 0.9891. Thus, the developed CCQDs-based chiral sensing system not only can achieve quantitative detection of L-Ile but also can determine the enantiomeric percentage in racemates.

The chiral recognition mechanism of CCQDs was further investigated by molecular modeling. Discovery Studio was used as computational method to draw the possible structure of CCQDs and to explore the binding mode and interaction energy between D-/L-Ile and CCQDs. The possible CCQDs structure was constructed according to the structure characteristics of CQDs and previously reported article [30,39]. The 2D structure of CCQD was prepared by ChemDraw software, then converted into 3D structure in the Discovery Studio Visualizer. The structure of CCQDs was corrected and optimized for minimized energy based on CHARMm force field with the help of molecular dynamics simulation function in the software. The complexation behaviors of CCQDs and D-/L-Ile were performed by CDOCKER docking programs. Three chiral recognition regions were planned to understand the difference in interactions between CCQDs and Ile enantiomers. As shown in Fig. 6 , due to the different stereoscopic configuration, L-Ile and D-Ile form different interactions with the chiral recognition sites in CCQDs. In a specific chiral recognition region, D-Ile forms conventional hydrogen bond interaction with L-Cys edge groups, while L-Ile not only forms conventional hydrogen bond interaction but also pi-donor hydrogen bond and carbon hydrogen bond interactions with L-Cys edge groups and internal aromatic system of CCQDs, which may explain the experimental phenomenon that L-Ile can induces the fluorescence enhancement of CCQDs. The binding energies of every complex system of CCQDs and D-/L-Ile were calculated and listed in Table 1 . It is found that L-Ile has higher binding energy than D-Ile with CCQDs. Since the difference in binding energy and interaction types, the chiral recognition occurs. There may be different types of chiral geometries in chiral nanomaterials, chirality of nanostructures could be found at atomic, nanoscale, submicron scale [16]. It is important to identify what chiral geometry is responsible for the chiral recognition. From the molecular docking model, it was found that the chiral recognition sites were concentrated in the residual chiral groups on the edge of CCQDs. No obvious nanoscale chiral geometries were identified. We speculate that it is the chirality at atomic scale resulting in the chiral recognition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-Ile in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed chiral sensing system for the analysis of L-Ile in real samples was tested. One of the energy drinks called MaiDong was obtained from local supermarket (RT-MART, Qingdao, CHINA) and used as analysis object. The energy drinks were detected without any pretreatment. According to PL measurement, the energy drinks have weak fluorescence signal at the detection wavelength of 450 nm (λex = 360 nm) as shown in Fig. 7 a, but the PL intensity of CCQDs has no obvious change in the presence of the energy drinks after deducting the background signal from the energy drinks, thus we judge that there is no L-Ile in the Maidong energy drinks. To verify our test result, HPLC method is used to determine the content of L-Ile in the real samples. The chromatograms of L-Ile standard and the Maidong energy drinks obtained on a commercial C18 column are shown in Fig. 7b, the characteristic peak of L-Ile does not appear in the chromatogram of real sample, thus a conclusion can be drawn that L-Ile is not added in the Maidong energy drinks. The test result from HPLC is in accord with the developed method, which indicates that the established fluorescence detection method towards L-Ile is reliable and practical.

Different concentrations of L-Ile were added into the energy drinks and measured by the established fluorescence detection method based on the previously obtained regression curve. The recoveries for detecting L-Ile in real samples ranged from 98.81% to 102.22% (Table 2 ). The satisfactory recoveries demonstrate that the developed fluorescence detection method is feasible for quickly and accurately determining L-Ile.

A fluorescent chiral recognition method based on CCQDs towards Ile enantiomers was developed in this work. The CCQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using L-Cys as chiral source and citric acid as carbon source. The CCQDs emitted blue fluorescence under UV excitation with maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 350 and 453 nm, respectively. The synthesized CCQDs have specific chiral recognition capacity for Ile enantiomers among 18 chiral amino acids under the detection condition of pH 7.4. L-Ile can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of CCQDs, but D-Ile has no obvious influence on the PL performance of CCQDs. This method has great prospect in real-time and rapid detection of L-Ile since the response speed of this chiral sensing system is fast, Ile enantiomers can be discriminated by CCQDs within 5 min, the enantioselectivity (IL/ID) can reach up to 2.2. Good linearity for detecting L-Ile was obtained over the concentration range from 0 to 30 mM with a LOD of 0.29 mM. The fluorescence intensity also increased linearly with the enantiomeric percentage of L-Ile in the mixture of Ile enantiomers. Thus, the developed method not only can achieve quantitative detection of L-Ile but also can determine the enantiomeric percentage in racemates. To better understand the chiral recognition mechanism, the possible structure of CCQDs and complexation behaviors of CCQDs and D-/L-Ile were constructed and investigated by molecular modeling. The chiral recognition can be explained by the difference in binding energy and interaction types between D-Ile and L-Ile with CCQDs. The established chiral recognition method was applied in detecting L-Ile in actual samples of functional drinks, the detection results were in consistent with the results obtained from high performance liquid chromatography, and the recoveries of standard addition were also satisfactory, which verified the reliability of the developed method.
The authors promise that this work is original, the experimental data is real and there is no academic misconduct. The same work is not submitted to other journals.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901766), the Talents of High Level Scientific Research Foundation, Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016), and the Support Plan on Science and Technology for Youth Innovation of Universities in Shandong Province (No. 2019KJM002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enantiomeric determination of drugs in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples by electrokinetic chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Casado, J. Valimana-Traverso, M.A. Garcia, M.L. Marina, Enantiomeric determination of drugs in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples by electrokinetic chromatography, Crit. Rev. Anal. Chem. 50 (2020) 554-584. https://doi.org/10.1080/10408347.2019.1670043
G.L. Marcone, E. Rosini, E. Crespi, L. Pollegioni, D-amino acids in foods, Appl. Microbiol. Biotechnol. 104 (2020) 555-574. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00253-019-10264-9

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Branched chain amino acids in metabolic disease

T. Kimura, A. Hesaka, Y. Isaka, D-Amino acids and kidney diseases, Clin. Exp. Nephrol. 24 (2020) 404-410. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10157-020-01862-3

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Opportunity with functional role of supplemental amino acids

G. Genchi, An overview on D-amino acids, Amino Acids 49 (2017) 1521-1533. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00726-017-2459-5

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enantioselective fluorescent recognition of free amino acids: challenges and opportunities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Pu, Enantioselective fluorescent recognition of free amino acids: challenges and opportunities, Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 21814-21828. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.202003969
